question: On June 6, 2023, an article by Carolyn Collins Petersen was published in Universe Today. This article mentions a team that produced a paper about their observations, linked at the bottom of the article. Find this paper. Under what NASA award number was the work performed by R. G. Arendt supported by?

There Are Hundreds of Mysterious Filaments at the Center of the Milky Way

\n
\n By Carolyn Collins Petersen - June 6, 2023 at 6:00 PM UTC | Milky Way\n
\n
\n\n \n\n
\n
\n

Several million years ago, the core of our galaxy experienced a powerful event. It blew out a huge bi-lobed bubble that blasted through the interstellar medium in two directions. Whatever it was, it released huge amounts of energy from the central supermassive black hole, Sagittarius A (Sgr A for short).

\n

These bubbles contain weird, spindly one-dimensional vertical filaments that emit radio signals of their own. In 1984, astronomer Farhad Yusef-Zadeh at Northwestern University and his collaborators were the first to observe these wispy trails. Now, they know of more than a thousand of them. They give off radio waves in the microwave portion of the electromagnetic spectrum.

\n

\n

Wait, There\'s More!

\n

Recently, the team found a smaller population of filaments near Sgr A*. However, these aren\'t vertical. Instead, they lie along the galactic plane and radiate away like spokes on a wheel. "It was a surprise to suddenly find a new population of structures that seem to be pointing in the direction of the black hole," Yusef-Zadeh said. "We have always been thinking about vertical filaments and their origin. I\'m used to them being vertical. I never considered there might be others along the plane."

\n

He and the team were stunned when they saw these radially stretched structures. Several million years ago, the core of our galaxy experienced a powerful event. It blew out a huge bi-lobed bubble that blasted through the interstellar medium in two directions. Whatever it was, it released huge amounts of energy from the central supermassive black hole, Sagittarius A (Sgr A for short). 
These bubbles contain weird, spindly one-dimensional vertical filaments that emit radio signals of their own. 
In 1984, astronomer Farhad Yusef-Zadeh at Northwestern University and his collaborators were the first to observe these wispy trails. 
Now, they know of more than a thousand of them. They give off radio waves in the microwave portion of the electromagnetic spectrum.
Recently, the team found a smaller population of filaments near Sgr A*. However, these aren’t vertical. Instead, they lie along the galactic plane and radiate away like spokes on a wheel. 
"It was a surprise to suddenly find a new population of structures that seem to be pointing in the direction of the black hole," Yusef-Zadeh said. 
"We have always been thinking about vertical filaments and their origin. I’m used to them being vertical. I never considered there might be others along the plane."

### 1. Task outcome (short version):
Farhad Yusef-Zadeh discovered that the Milky Way contains hundreds of long, strange filaments that emit bright radio waves, suggesting they may be remnants of past activities related to the supermassive black hole at the galaxy's center. These filaments are at least ten times more numerous than previously thought.

### 2. Task outcome (extremely detailed version):
The filament structures identified in the Milky Way by Farhad Yusef-Zadeh are intriguing long threads of gas that can be observed through their radio wave emissions. These filaments extend over vast distances and were initially noted for their unusual forms, which twisting and interacting with the surrounding magnetic fields. 

The research indicates that these filaments display a bright radio-wave emission, leading to speculation regarding their origin and role within the galaxy. One theory is that they may be leftover products from activities associated with the supermassive black hole, known as Sagittarius A*, located at the center of the Milky Way. The sheer number of these structures is significant; Yusef-Zadeh's findings suggest that the filament population is at least ten times more extensive than scientists had previously realized.

This discovery has implications for understanding the dynamic processes at play in the Galactic center and may impact the broader field of astrophysics by enhancing comprehension of cosmic structure and the behavior of matter around supermassive black holes. The filaments could also provide insights into galactic formation and the interplay between mysterious dark matter and baryonic matter.

The research paper by Farhad Yusef-Zadeh and colleagues investigates the population of non-thermal radio filaments (NRFs) in the Galactic Centre. It presents a catalog of 57 candidate radio and infrared sources associated with these filaments, supported by recent MeerKAT observations. The study explores possible correlations between compact radio sources, infrared stars, and NRFs, suggesting a potential physical link that may involve cosmic-ray-driven winds and interactions with stellar winds.

The paper titled "The population of Galactic Centre filaments – III. Candidate radio and stellar sources" expands on the presence of non-thermal radio filaments (NRFs) in the central regions of the Milky Way, where unusual cosmic activities occur. Utilizing MeerKAT radio observations, the authors identified a sample of 57 compact radio sources associated with the NRFs, significantly higher than random chance predictions. 

The study highlights an observed correlation between bright infrared (IR) stars and NRFs, indicated by similar latitude distributions, suggesting co-existence within the same spatial region. This correlation prompted the authors to analyze spectral energy distributions (SEDs) from archival IR data (2MASS and Spitzer) for individual stellar sources adjoining these radio sources.

The paper proposes that the NRFs might result from a combination of nuclear wind interactions and stellar winds from mass-losing stars, producing cometary tail-like structures. The authors outline the need for further high-resolution observations to explore this connection and to validate the cometary tail model of filamentary origins. 

This research is significant for understanding the dynamic environments of the inner Milky Way, an area highly influenced by the supermassive black hole Sgr A*. The interactions between cosmic rays, stellar winds, and the interstellar medium in this region have far-reaching implications for astrophysical models regarding galaxy formation and evolution. The findings contribute to a deeper comprehension of cosmic phenomena and the role of stellar and nuclear activities in shaping our galaxy's central region.

### 3. Additional context (if relevant):
